content.full_text:
.

State of California
Also filed 84303

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ... 1109 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance .. Administration .. Executive/Legal .. Enforcement .. Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5640              322-5901            322-6441              322-6444

April 28, 1981


Eileen E. Padberg
Robert Nelson and Associates, Inc.
12971 Pitch Avenue
Irvine, CA  92714

Dear Ms. Padberg:

This is in response to your letter of March 20, 1981, and my
subsequent telephone conversation with you in which you asked
several questions regarding a group of employees forming a
Political Action Committee (PAC). Your questions were:

1. Are there any prohibitions as to how the committee may
   raise money?

2. Are there any prohibitions with regard to who may be a
   member of the committee?

3. How does the "PAC" report income raised by holding events
   or sales?

4. How are operating expenses reported?

5. Are there any prohibitions on how money may be spent?

A Political Action Committee (PAC) means a committee within the
meaning of Government Code Section 82013(a). There are currently
no prohibitions in the Political Reform Act as interpreted by
the Commission on who may be a "member" or contributor to a "PAC".
There are no prohibitions regarding how funds may be raised or
spent, except that no anonymous contributions of $100 or more in
a calendar year may be taken into the "PAC"'s funds (Government
Code Section 84302). In addition, no monetary contribution or
expenditures of $100 or more may be made or received in cash
(Government Code Section 84300).

All contributions received and expenditures made by a "PAC" must
be reported and all contributions from a single source of $100
or more and expenditures to a single source of $100 or more must
be itemized (Government Code Section 84211). In the case where
contributions are received from proceeds of a sale or fundraising
event and it is determined that no individual contribution of $100

Eileen E. Padberg                                                    -2-


or more was received, the "PAC" may report the proceeds as a total
amount. The suggested method for reporting such proceeds is to
list the total proceeds on Schedule A - amount received column -
with a note as to how the funds were collected. An example would
be as follows:

Date           Full Name and Address of Contributor              Amount
Received

5/19/81        Proceeds from bake sale (No individual
               contributions received of $100 or more).          393.00

I have enclosed FPPC Regulation 18419 and a copy of the 1981 Informational Manual on Campaign Disclosure which I feel may be of some
use to you. If you need any additional materials or information,
please contact me.

                                                Sincerely,


                                                [signature]
                                                Lynn Montgomery
                                                Political Reform Consultant

Enclosure

March 20, 1981


Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, Ca 95814

Re: Request for Opinion

With regard to a political action committee, a group of
employees (not associated with the employees organization)
have established a political action committee and have filed
as such.

There are some questions which remain to be answered:

     1. Are there any prohibitions with regard to how the
        money is raised? (They are currently involved in
        bake sales, ski trips, tear gas classes, etc.)

     2. Are there any prohibitions with regard to membership
        on the committee? (Can a member of the association's
        board of directors participate?)

     3. Reporting requirements with relation to how to report
        the income into the PAC? (For example, $399 raised
        from Christmas Boutique sale, $357 raised from tear
        gas classes. No single contribution into the fund is
        more than $100.)

     4. How are operating expenses reported and what are
        prohibitions on those expenses? (Currently, all work
        is volunteer except for bookkeeper's time.)

     5. Are there any prohibitions with regard to how money
        is spent? (Currently, they use funds for
        contributions to events, members of local Board of
        Supervisors, members of State Legislature. Reports
        are made to Secretary of State on Form #450.)


18071 Fitch • Irvine, California 92714 • (714)857-1010

Fair Political Practices Commission
March 20, 1981
Page 2


6.  Must the committee have any specific officers,
bylaws?

Your written response would be extremely valuable for the
committee's guidelines.

Sincerely,

[Signature]

Eileen E. Padberg
Vice President

EEP:ins

cc:  Linda Stewart
John H. Sawyer, Gen. Mgr.
Jim Jeu Devine
